1:
"Finally, brethren, pray for us, that the word of the Lord may have free course, and be glorified, even as it is with you:" The witnesses note Paul's request for prayer that the word may run and be glorified. On "pray for us, that the word of the Lord may have free course," the grammatical voices note the petition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's request for their intercession: "pray for us, that the word of the Lord may have free course (trecho — may run, speed onward swiftly, like a runner)" — that the gospel may advance unhindered, spreading rapidly.

On "and be glorified," the witnesses note the second aim. Barnes and Calvin note the further desire: "and be glorified (honoured, received with honour)" — that the word may not only spread but be honoured wherever it goes, received and prized; its free course and its glorification go together.

On "even as it is with you," the eastern Cyril and puritan Henry note the precedent: "even as it is with you" — as the word had run and been glorified among the Thessalonians, so Paul asks that it may everywhere; their own experience is the pattern of his prayer. The verse displays Paul's request for prayer that the word may run and be glorified.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's request — "pray for us, that the word of the Lord may have free course (may run swiftly)," the gospel advancing unhindered (Barnes, Robertson); "and be glorified (honoured, prized)," received with honour wherever it spreads (Barnes, Calvin); "even as it is with you," the Thessalonians' own experience the pattern (Cyril, Henry). The verse displays the request for prayer. The verse marks the petition: pray for us. The verse displays the desire: free course. The verse displays the honour: glorified. The verse displays the pattern: as with you. The request displays Paul seeking prayer for the gospel's advance. The verse displays the word: running. The verse displays the glory: received. The verse displays the precedent: among them. Paul's request for prayer that the word may run and be glorified displays the missionary heart that closes his letter: he asks the Thessalonians to pray that the gospel may speed onward unhindered and be received with honour everywhere — as it had been among them. The verse displays the prayer for the word, joined to a prayer for deliverance (v.2).
2:
"And that we may be delivered from unreasonable and wicked men: for all men have not faith." The brief verse notes the prayer for deliverance from wicked men who lack faith. The grammatical voices note the second petition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's request for personal deliverance: "that we may be delivered (rescued) from unreasonable (atopon — out of place, perverse, absurd) and wicked (evil, malicious) men" — from the perverse and hostile opponents who threatened his work and safety (likely the Jews at Corinth, Acts 18).

On "unreasonable and wicked men," the witnesses note their character. Barnes and Calvin note the description: "unreasonable" (perverse, unprincipled, beyond the bounds of reason or fairness) and "wicked" (active in evil) — men whose opposition was both irrational and malicious.

On "for all men have not faith," the grammatical-historical note the explanation: "all men have not faith (the faith)" — not all receive or hold the gospel; the opposition arises because faith is not universal, and the faithless resist the truth. The verse displays the prayer for deliverance from wicked men who lack faith.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's request — "that we may be delivered (rescued) from unreasonable (perverse) and wicked men," from the hostile opponents threatening his work (the Jews at Corinth, Barnes, Robertson); their character "unreasonable" (beyond reason) and "wicked" (malicious), opposition irrational and evil (Barnes, Calvin); "for all men have not faith," the opposition arising because faith is not universal (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prayer for deliverance. The verse marks the petition: be delivered. The verse displays the foes: unreasonable and wicked. The verse displays the character: perverse, malicious. The verse displays the explanation: all have not faith. The prayer displays Paul seeking rescue from faithless opponents. The verse displays the deliverance: sought. The verse displays the men: wicked. The verse displays the faith: not universal. The prayer for deliverance from wicked men who lack faith displays the second request of Paul's intercession: that he be rescued from the perverse and malicious men who opposed his work — for not all receive the faith, and the faithless resist the gospel. The verse displays the prayer for deliverance, answered by confidence in the Lord's faithfulness (v.3).
3:
"But the Lord is faithful, who shall stablish you, and keep you from evil." The witnesses note the assurance that the faithful Lord will stablish and keep them. On "the Lord is faithful," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the turn from faithless men to the faithful Lord: "but the Lord is faithful (pistos — trustworthy, true to his promises)" — over against the men who "have not faith" (v.2), the Lord is full of faithfulness; there is a play on the word.

On "who shall stablish you," the witnesses note the first promise. Barnes and Calvin note the assurance: the faithful Lord "shall stablish (sterixei — make firm, strengthen, confirm) you" — he will steady and strengthen them, the very thing Paul prayed for (2:17); what believers need, the faithful Lord supplies.

On "and keep you from evil," the eastern Chrysostom notes the second promise: "and keep (guard, preserve) you from evil (apo tou ponerou — from the evil one, Satan, or from evil generally)" — the Lord will guard them from the evil one's assaults; whether "evil" is the wicked one himself or evil in general, the faithful Lord is their protector. The verse displays the assurance that the faithful Lord will stablish and keep them.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the turn — "the Lord is faithful (trustworthy, true)," over against the faithless men, a play on the word (Barnes, Robertson); "who shall stablish (strengthen, confirm) you," the faithful Lord supplying what Paul prayed for (Barnes, Calvin); "and keep (guard) you from evil (the evil one, or evil generally)," the Lord their protector against Satan's assaults (Chrysostom). The verse displays the assurance. The verse marks the faithfulness: the Lord is faithful. The verse displays the first promise: stablish you. The verse displays the second: keep from evil. The verse displays the contrast: faithful Lord, faithless men. The assurance displays the faithful Lord steadying and guarding them. The verse displays the Lord: faithful. The verse displays the stablishing: sure. The verse displays the keeping: from evil. The assurance that the faithful Lord will stablish and keep them displays the comfort answering the threat of wicked men: though not all men have faith, the Lord is faithful — and he will strengthen the Thessalonians and guard them from the evil one. The verse displays the faithful Lord, grounding Paul's confidence in their obedience (v.4).
4:
"And we have confidence in the Lord touching you, that ye both do and will do the things which we command you." The brief verse notes Paul's confidence in their present and continued obedience. The grammatical voices note the ground of confidence. Barnes and Robertson note that Paul's confidence rests not in them but in the Lord: "we have confidence in the Lord touching you (concerning you)" — his trust in their obedience is grounded in the Lord, who works in them; confidence "in the Lord," not in human steadfastness alone.

On "that ye both do and will do the things which we command you," the witnesses note the obedience. Barnes and Calvin note the substance: "that ye both do (now obey) and will do (will continue to obey) the things which we command you" — he is confident of both their present and their future obedience to his apostolic instructions; the charges that follow (vv.6-15) are given on this confidence.

The grammatical-historical note the tact: Paul prefaces his commands with an expression of confidence, gently disposing them to obey by assuming they will. The verse displays Paul's confidence in their present and continued obedience.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's confidence grounded in the Lord — "we have confidence in the Lord touching you," his trust resting on the Lord who works in them (Barnes, Robertson); "that ye both do and will do the things which we command you," confident of present and future obedience (Barnes, Calvin); prefacing his commands with confidence, gently disposing them to obey (grammatical-historical). The verse displays his confidence. The verse marks the trust: in the Lord. The verse displays the present: ye do. The verse displays the future: will do. The verse displays the substance: what we command. The confidence displays Paul trusting their obedience in the Lord. The verse displays the confidence: in the Lord. The verse displays the doing: present and future. The verse displays the command: his. Paul's confidence in their present and continued obedience displays the trust that frames his charges: he is confident, in the Lord who works in them, that the Thessalonians both now obey and will go on obeying his apostolic commands. The verse displays the apostle's confidence, sealed by a prayer for their love and patience (v.5).
5:
"And the Lord direct your hearts into the love of God, and into the patient waiting for Christ." The witnesses note the prayer that the Lord direct their hearts to God's love and Christ's patience. On "the Lord direct your hearts," the grammatical voices note the petition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's prayer: "the Lord direct (kateuthynai — make straight, guide, lead) your hearts" — that the Lord would guide and incline their inmost affections in the right path; the heart needs divine direction.

On "into the love of God," the witnesses note the first object. Barnes and Calvin note the goal: "into the love of God" — whether God's love to them (that they may rest in it) or their love to God (that they may grow in it), most taking it as their love for God, into which the Lord directs their hearts.

On "and into the patient waiting for Christ," interpreters note the phrase. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the patient waiting for Christ (hypomonen tou Christou)" may mean either the patient expectation of Christ's coming, or (more literally) the patience/steadfast endurance of Christ — the endurance that Christ himself showed and gives; either the patient hope of his return, or Christ-like steadfastness. The verse displays the prayer that the Lord direct their hearts to God's love and Christ's patience.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's prayer — "the Lord direct (guide, make straight) your hearts," the Lord guiding their inmost affections (Barnes, Robertson); "into the love of God," their love to God (or God's love to them), into which their hearts are directed (Barnes, Calvin); "the patient waiting for Christ (the patience of Christ)," either patient expectation of his coming or Christ-like endurance (Barnes, Ellicott). The verse displays the prayer. The verse marks the petition: direct your hearts. The verse displays the first: the love of God. The verse displays the second: the patience of Christ. The verse displays the guidance: divine. The prayer displays the Lord guiding their hearts to love and patience. The verse displays the directing: of hearts. The verse displays the love: of God. The verse displays the patience: of Christ. The prayer that the Lord direct their hearts to God's love and Christ's patience displays the close of Paul's confident words: he prays that the Lord would guide the Thessalonians' hearts into the love of God and into the patient endurance (and expectation) of Christ. The verse displays the prayer for love and patience, before the charge against disorder (vv.6-15).
6:
"Now we command you, brethren, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that ye withdraw yourselves from every brother that walketh disorderly, and not after the tradition which he received of us." The witnesses note the command to withdraw from the disorderly brother. On "we command you... in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the authority. Barnes and Robertson note the solemn weight of the charge: "we command (parangellomen — charge, enjoin with authority) you... in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ" — Paul gives this not as advice but as an authoritative apostolic command, backed by Christ's own name.

On "that ye withdraw yourselves from every brother that walketh disorderly," the witnesses note the discipline. Barnes and Calvin note the charge: "withdraw yourselves (stellesthai — draw back, keep aloof, avoid) from every brother that walketh disorderly (ataktos — out of rank, idly, irregularly)" — the "disorderly" being chiefly the idle busybodies (v.11) who, expecting Christ's imminent coming, had quit work; the church is to keep its distance from such.

On "not after the tradition which he received of us," the puritan Henry notes the standard: the disorderly walk is "not after the tradition (the apostolic teaching and example) which he received of us" — their conduct departs from the rule Paul had delivered; the apostolic tradition is the measure of orderly walking. The verse displays the command to withdraw from the disorderly brother.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the solemn charge — "we command (enjoin with authority)... in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ," an authoritative apostolic command (Barnes, Robertson); "withdraw yourselves (keep aloof) from every brother that walketh disorderly (out of rank, idly)," chiefly the idle busybodies (Barnes, Calvin); "not after the tradition which he received of us," departing from the apostolic rule (Henry). The verse displays the command. The verse marks the authority: in Christ's name. The verse displays the discipline: withdraw. The verse displays the disorderly: walking idly. The verse displays the standard: the tradition. The command displays the church keeping aloof from the disorderly. The verse displays the command: authoritative. The verse displays the withdrawal: charged. The verse displays the disorder: against the tradition. The command to withdraw from the disorderly brother displays the discipline Paul enjoins: in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ he charges the church to keep aloof from every brother who walks disorderly — idly and out of rank — contrary to the apostolic tradition delivered to them. The verse displays the charge against disorder, grounded in Paul's own example (vv.7-9).
7:
"For yourselves know how ye ought to follow us: for we behaved not ourselves disorderly among you;" The brief verse notes Paul's own orderly example as the pattern. The grammatical voices note the appeal to their knowledge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul points to his own conduct as the model: "yourselves know how ye ought to follow (imitate) us: for we behaved not ourselves disorderly (ataktos — idly, out of rank) among you" — they knew, from his example, the orderly, industrious life they should imitate.

On "follow us," the witnesses note the imitation. Barnes and Calvin note that Paul holds up his own example as the standard of orderly walking; the rule he commands (v.6) he himself first kept, so that they have a living pattern to follow.

The grammatical-historical note the consistency: Paul could enjoin diligence because he had practised it; his preaching and his conduct agreed, and his blameless example gave weight to his command. The verse displays Paul's own orderly example as the pattern.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul points to his conduct — "yourselves know how ye ought to follow (imitate) us: for we behaved not ourselves disorderly (idly) among you," they knowing from his example the orderly life to imitate (Barnes, Robertson); his own example the standard, the rule he commands he first kept (Barnes, Calvin); his preaching and conduct agreeing, giving weight to his command (grammatical-historical). The verse displays his example. The verse marks the appeal: ye know. The verse displays the pattern: follow us. The verse displays the conduct: not disorderly. The verse displays the consistency: practice matched preaching. The example displays Paul's orderly life as the model. The verse displays the following: of Paul. The verse displays the order: kept. The verse displays the example: blameless. Paul's own orderly example as the pattern displays the ground of his charge against idleness: the Thessalonians knew how they ought to imitate him, for he had not behaved disorderly or idly among them — his own industrious, orderly life being the living standard he set before them. The verse displays the apostle's example, shown in his self-supporting toil (v.8).
8:
"Neither did we eat any man's bread for nought; but wrought with labour and travail night and day, that we might not be chargeable to any of you:" The brief verse notes Paul's self-supporting toil so as not to burden them. The grammatical voices note the disclaimer and the labour. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's example in detail: "neither did we eat any man's bread for nought (without paying, as a free charge)" — he took no free maintenance; rather "wrought (worked) with labour and travail (kopos kai mochthos — toil and hardship) night and day" — labouring at his trade (tentmaking).

On "night and day," the witnesses note the constancy. Barnes and Calvin note that Paul worked "night and day" — constant, wearying toil to support himself, so that his preaching might cost the church nothing; the same self-denial described in the first epistle (1 Thessalonians 2:9).

On "that we might not be chargeable to any of you," the grammatical-historical note the motive: he did this "that we might not be chargeable (a burden, a financial charge) to any of you" — that none might be burdened, and that he might leave an example of industry. The verse displays Paul's self-supporting toil so as not to burden them.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's example — "neither did we eat any man's bread for nought (without paying)," taking no free maintenance, but "wrought with labour and travail (toil and hardship) night and day," labouring at his trade (Barnes, Robertson); constant wearying toil to support himself, the same self-denial as 1 Thessalonians 2:9 (Barnes, Calvin); "that we might not be chargeable (a burden) to any of you," that none be burdened (grammatical-historical). The verse displays his toil. The verse marks the disclaimer: no bread for nought. The verse displays the labour: toil and travail. The verse displays the constancy: night and day. The verse displays the motive: not chargeable. The toil displays Paul working to burden no one. The verse displays the bread: paid for. The verse displays the working: night and day. The verse displays the charge: avoided. Paul's self-supporting toil so as not to burden them displays the example behind his charge against idleness: he ate no man's bread without paying, but laboured night and day at his trade, in toil and hardship, that he might be a burden to none — a living pattern of industry. The verse displays the apostle's labour, given as an example though he had the right to support (v.9).
9:
"Not because we have not power, but to make ourselves an ensample unto you to follow us." The brief verse notes Paul foregoing his right to set an example. The grammatical voices note the right he had. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's careful qualification: "not because we have not power (exousia — the right, the authority)" — he did have the right, as an apostle, to be supported by the churches (1 Corinthians 9:4-14); he did not forego it because he lacked the claim.

On "but to make ourselves an ensample unto you," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note the reason he waived his right: "but to make ourselves an ensample (typon — a pattern, model) unto you to follow (imitate) us" — he supported himself precisely to set an example of industry, that they might copy his diligence and not be idle.

The grammatical-historical note the self-denial: Paul gave up a lawful right for their good, that his example might teach more powerfully than mere words; the foregone right made the lesson the stronger. The verse displays Paul foregoing his right to set an example.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's qualification — "not because we have not power (the right, the authority)," he did have the apostolic right to support (1 Corinthians 9, Barnes, Robertson); "but to make ourselves an ensample (pattern) unto you to follow us," waiving the right to set an example of industry (Barnes, Calvin); giving up a lawful right for their good, the foregone right strengthening the lesson (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the foregone right. The verse marks the right: we have power. The verse displays the purpose: an ensample. The verse displays the aim: that they follow. The verse displays the self-denial: right waived. The example displays Paul foregoing support to teach industry. The verse displays the power: held. The verse displays the ensample: set. The verse displays the following: invited. Paul foregoing his right to set an example displays the deliberateness of his self-denial: he had the apostolic right to be supported, yet he waived it and laboured with his own hands precisely to make himself a pattern of industry for the Thessalonians to imitate. The verse displays the apostle's example, enforcing the rule that the idle should not eat (v.10).
10:
"For even when we were with you, this we commanded you, that if any would not work, neither should he eat." The witnesses note the rule that he who will not work should not eat. On "even when we were with you, this we commanded you," the grammatical voices note the prior charge. Barnes and Robertson note that the rule was no new invention: "even when we were with you, this we commanded you" — Paul had laid down this rule already, during his ministry at Thessalonica; the present letter only enforces what he had taught.

On "if any would not work," the witnesses note the key distinction. Barnes and Calvin note the careful wording: "if any would not (is unwilling to, refuses to) work" — not those who cannot find work or are unable, but those who will not; the rule targets willful idleness, not misfortune; the refusal of the will is the point.

On "neither should he eat," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the rule: "neither should he eat" — let him not be maintained at others' expense; the able-bodied idler has no claim on the charity of the church; he must work to eat. The verse displays the rule that he who will not work should not eat.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the prior charge — "even when we were with you, this we commanded you," the rule already laid down in his ministry, now enforced (Barnes, Robertson); "if any would not (is unwilling to) work," targeting willful idleness, not those unable, the refusal of the will the point (Barnes, Calvin); "neither should he eat," the able idler having no claim on the church's charity (Wesley). The verse displays the rule. The verse marks the charge: commanded already. The verse displays the condition: will not work. The verse displays the distinction: unwilling, not unable. The verse displays the rule: neither eat. The rule displays the willful idler denied maintenance. The verse displays the command: prior. The verse displays the unwillingness: the point. The verse displays the eating: forfeited. The rule that he who will not work should not eat displays the principle Paul had taught and now enforces: the man who is unwilling to work — not he who cannot, but he who refuses — has no claim to be fed at others' expense; idleness forfeits the right to bread. The verse displays the rule against idleness, applied to the busybodies among them (v.11).
11:
"For we hear that there are some which walk among you disorderly, working not at all, but are busybodies." The brief verse notes the report of idle busybodies among them. The grammatical voices note the occasion. Barnes and Robertson note the reason for the charge: "we hear (it is reported) that there are some which walk among you disorderly (ataktos — out of rank, idly)" — a specific problem had arisen in the church; some were living in idleness.

On "working not at all, but are busybodies," the witnesses note the wordplay. Barnes and Calvin note the pointed contrast (a play on words in the Greek, ergazomenous / periergazomenous): "working not at all, but are busybodies (meddlers)" — they did no proper work of their own, yet were busy meddling in others' affairs; idleness breeds officious interference.

The grammatical-historical note the likely cause: the idleness probably sprang from their mistaken excitement about Christ's imminent coming, which led them to abandon work; idleness then turned to meddling. The verse displays the report of idle busybodies among them.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the occasion — "we hear (it is reported) that there are some which walk... disorderly (idly)," a specific problem of idleness (Barnes, Robertson); the wordplay "working not at all, but are busybodies (meddlers)," doing no work yet busy in others' affairs, idleness breeding meddling (Barnes, Calvin); the cause likely their excitement over Christ's imminent coming (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the report. The verse marks the disorder: walking idly. The verse displays the idleness: working not at all. The verse displays the meddling: busybodies. The verse displays the wordplay: not busy but busybodies. The report displays idlers turned meddlers in the church. The verse displays the hearing: reported. The verse displays the idleness: total. The verse displays the meddling: officious. The report of idle busybodies among them displays the concrete occasion of Paul's charge: it was reported that some in the church walked disorderly, doing no proper work at all, yet busying themselves as meddlers in others' affairs — idleness having turned to officious interference. The verse displays the disorderly idlers, whom Paul commands to quiet industry (v.12).
12:
"Now them that are such we command and exhort by our Lord Jesus Christ, that with quietness they work, and eat their own bread." The brief verse notes the charge to the idle to work quietly and eat their own bread. The grammatical voices note the authority and tenderness. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's balanced address to the idlers: "we command (charge with authority) and exhort (parakaloumen — entreat, encourage) by our Lord Jesus Christ" — both commanding and entreating, joining apostolic authority with brotherly persuasion, in Christ's name.

On "with quietness they work," the witnesses note the remedy. Barnes and Calvin note the cure for the meddling idleness: "that with quietness (hesychias — quietly, peaceably, settling down) they work" — over against the restless meddling (v.11), they are to settle to quiet, steady labour; quietness is set against busybody bustle.

On "and eat their own bread," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the end: "and eat their own bread" — bread earned by their own labour, not another's; the honest fruit of their own work, not charity or others' toil. The verse displays the charge to the idle to work quietly and eat their own bread.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's balanced address — "we command (charge) and exhort (entreat) by our Lord Jesus Christ," joining authority with persuasion in Christ's name (Barnes, Robertson); "with quietness (quietly) they work," settling to steady labour over against meddling (Barnes, Calvin); "and eat their own bread," bread earned by their own toil, not another's (Wesley). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the address: command and exhort. The verse displays the remedy: with quietness work. The verse displays the contrast: against meddling. The verse displays the end: their own bread. The charge displays the idlers called to quiet labour. The verse displays the working: quiet. The verse displays the quietness: required. The verse displays the bread: their own. The charge to the idle to work quietly and eat their own bread displays the remedy Paul prescribes: he both commands and entreats the idlers, in the name of the Lord Jesus, to settle down to quiet, steady labour and eat the bread their own hands have earned. The verse displays the cure for idleness, with a word to the faithful (v.13).
13:
"But ye, brethren, be not weary in well doing." The brief verse notes the exhortation to the faithful not to grow weary in well doing. The grammatical voices note the turn to the rest. Barnes and Robertson note Paul turns from the idlers to the faithful body: "but ye, brethren, be not weary (ekkakesete — lose heart, grow weary, faint) in well doing (doing good)" — the well-behaved are not to grow tired of doing right, whether of their honest labour or of their charity.

On "well doing," the witnesses note the scope. Barnes and Calvin note that "well doing" may include both persevering in their own good conduct and continuing in deeds of kindness — perhaps especially not letting the abuse of charity by the idle discourage them from genuine generosity to the truly needy.

The grammatical-historical note the caution: the danger was that, weary of the idlers' imposition, the faithful might grow cold in all good works; Paul guards against this — the misconduct of some must not chill the well-doing of the rest. The verse displays the exhortation to the faithful not to grow weary in well doing.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul turns to the faithful — "be not weary (lose heart, faint) in well doing (doing good)," not growing tired of doing right (Barnes, Robertson); "well doing" including both persevering in good conduct and continuing in kindness, especially not letting the idlers' abuse discourage genuine charity (Barnes, Calvin); the misconduct of some not to chill the well-doing of the rest (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the exhortation. The verse marks the address: but ye, brethren. The verse displays the charge: be not weary. The verse displays the scope: in well doing. The verse displays the caution: against discouragement. The exhortation displays the faithful urged to persevere in good. The verse displays the weariness: forbidden. The verse displays the well doing: continued. The verse displays the faithful: encouraged. The exhortation to the faithful not to grow weary in well doing displays Paul's care for the rest of the church: turning from the idlers, he urges the well-behaved not to lose heart or grow tired of doing good — lest the abuse of charity by the idle chill their genuine well-doing. The verse displays the call to persevere in good, with a word on disciplining the disobedient (v.14).
14:
"And if any man obey not our word by this epistle, note that man, and have no company with him, that he may be ashamed." The witnesses note the discipline of marking and avoiding the disobedient. On "if any man obey not our word by this epistle," the grammatical voices note the case. Barnes and Robertson note the contingency: "if any man obey not (refuses to obey) our word by this epistle" — if any, after this letter, still persists in disobedience to the apostolic command; the discipline is for the persistently disobedient.

On "note that man, and have no company with him," the witnesses note the action. Barnes and Calvin note the twofold step: "note (semeiousthe — mark, take note of, set a mark upon) that man, and have no company with him (do not associate with him, keep aloof)" — the church is to single him out and withdraw ordinary fellowship, that he feel the weight of his fault.

On "that he may be ashamed," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the aim: the discipline is "that he may be ashamed (be put to shame, brought to repentance)" — the goal is not destruction but reformation; the shame is meant to bring him to his senses and back to right conduct. The verse displays the discipline of marking and avoiding the disobedient.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the case — "if any man obey not (refuses) our word by this epistle," the persistently disobedient after the letter (Barnes, Robertson); the twofold step "note (mark) that man, and have no company with him," singling him out and withdrawing fellowship (Barnes, Calvin); "that he may be ashamed (brought to repentance)," the aim reformation not destruction (Wesley). The verse displays the discipline. The verse marks the case: disobeys the epistle. The verse displays the marking: note that man. The verse displays the avoidance: no company. The verse displays the aim: that he be ashamed. The discipline displays the church marking the disobedient to reclaim him. The verse displays the disobedience: noted. The verse displays the company: withdrawn. The verse displays the shame: corrective. The discipline of marking and avoiding the disobedient displays the church's remedy for persistent disorder: if any will not obey the apostolic word in this letter, the church is to mark him and withdraw ordinary fellowship — that he may be brought to shame and so to repentance. The verse displays the corrective discipline, tempered by brotherly love (v.15).
15:
"Yet count him not as an enemy, but admonish him as a brother." The brief verse notes the limit of discipline: to admonish, not treat as an enemy. The grammatical voices note the tempering of discipline. Barnes and Robertson note the gracious limit: "yet count him not as an enemy" — the withdrawal of fellowship (v.14) is not enmity; the disobedient brother is not to be treated as a foe, hated or cast off as hopeless.

On "but admonish him as a brother," the witnesses note the right spirit. Barnes and Calvin note the aim: "but admonish (noutheteite — warn, counsel, set right) him as a brother" — even in discipline, he remains a brother; the correction is to be done in love, with the aim of reclaiming him, not punishing or destroying.

The puritan Henry notes the balance: church discipline must hold together firmness and love — firm enough to mark the fault and withdraw fellowship, yet loving enough to treat the offender still as a brother to be won back, not an enemy to be destroyed. The verse displays the limit of discipline: to admonish, not treat as an enemy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gracious limit — "count him not as an enemy," the withdrawal not enmity, the brother not treated as a foe (Barnes, Robertson); "but admonish (warn, counsel) him as a brother," correcting in love to reclaim him (Barnes, Calvin); discipline holding firmness and love together, the offender still a brother to be won (Henry). The verse displays the tempered discipline. The verse marks the limit: not as an enemy. The verse displays the spirit: admonish as a brother. The verse displays the aim: to reclaim. The verse displays the balance: firmness and love. The limit displays discipline tempered by brotherly love. The verse displays the enemy: not so counted. The verse displays the brother: still. The verse displays the admonition: loving. The limit of discipline, to admonish and not treat as an enemy, displays the loving spirit that must govern correction: even the disciplined brother is not to be counted a foe, but admonished as a brother — the aim of all discipline being to reclaim him, not to destroy. The verse displays the love within discipline, sealed by a prayer for peace (v.16).
16:
"Now the Lord of peace himself give you peace always by all means. The Lord be with you all." The witnesses note the prayer to the Lord of peace for peace and his presence. On "the Lord of peace himself give you peace always," the grammatical voices note the title and petition. Barnes and Robertson note the fitting title after the discord he addressed: "the Lord of peace himself (the Lord who is the author and source of peace) give you peace always (at all times)" — Paul prays that Christ, the very source of peace, would grant them continual peace.

On "by all means," the witnesses note the comprehensiveness. Barnes and Calvin note the breadth: "by all means (in every way, in every circumstance)" — peace of every kind and in every situation: peace with God, peace among themselves (over against their divisions), and peace within; total and unbroken peace.

On "the Lord be with you all," the puritan Henry notes the crowning blessing: "the Lord be with you all" — the presence of the Lord is the sum of all blessing and the source of all peace; with him present, they have all they need. The verse displays the prayer to the Lord of peace for peace and his presence.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fitting title — "the Lord of peace himself (the author of peace) give you peace always (at all times)," Christ the source granting continual peace (Barnes, Robertson); "by all means (in every way and circumstance)," peace of every kind — with God, among themselves, within (Barnes, Calvin); "the Lord be with you all," his presence the sum of blessing and source of peace (Henry). The verse displays the prayer for peace. The verse marks the title: the Lord of peace. The verse displays the petition: peace always. The verse displays the breadth: by all means. The verse displays the blessing: the Lord with you. The prayer displays Christ the peace-giver granting peace and presence. The verse displays the peace: always. The verse displays the means: all. The verse displays the presence: the Lord's. The prayer to the Lord of peace for peace and his presence displays the close of Paul's exhortations: he prays that Christ, the very Lord of peace, would give them peace at all times and in every way, and that the Lord himself would be with them all. The verse displays the blessing of peace, before the apostle's own signature (v.17).
17:
"The salutation of Paul with mine own hand, which is the token in every epistle: so I write." The witnesses note Paul's own-hand salutation, the mark of authenticity. On "the salutation of Paul with mine own hand," the grammatical voices note the autograph. Barnes and Robertson note that Paul, who dictated the letter, now adds the closing greeting in his own handwriting: "the salutation of Paul with mine own hand" — his personal signature, authenticating the letter as genuinely his.

On "which is the token in every epistle," the witnesses note the safeguard. Barnes and Calvin note the purpose: "which is the token (semeion — the sign, mark) in every epistle" — this autograph greeting is his standard mark of genuineness; given the danger of forged letters in his name (2:2), Paul's own hand was the sign by which his true letters could be known.

On "so I write," the grammatical-historical note the specimen: "so I write" — perhaps pointing to the very style or form of his handwriting, the recognizable character of his autograph, that they might distinguish his genuine letters from forgeries. The verse displays Paul's own-hand salutation, the mark of authenticity.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul adds the closing in his own hand — "the salutation of Paul with mine own hand," his personal signature authenticating the letter (Barnes, Robertson); "which is the token (sign) in every epistle," his standard mark of genuineness against forged letters (2:2, Barnes, Calvin); "so I write," pointing to the recognizable form of his autograph (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the autograph. The verse marks the salutation: his own hand. The verse displays the token: in every epistle. The verse displays the purpose: against forgery. The verse displays the specimen: so I write. The signature displays Paul authenticating his letter. The verse displays the hand: his own. The verse displays the token: of genuineness. The verse displays the writing: his mark. Paul's own-hand salutation, the mark of authenticity, displays the apostle's safeguard against forgery: having dictated the letter, he signs the closing greeting himself — the token in every epistle by which his genuine letters may be known, against the false letters that had troubled them. The verse displays the authenticating signature, sealed by the grace of Christ (v.18).
18:
"The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen." The witnesses note the closing benediction of grace upon them all. On "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the blessing. Barnes and Robertson note Paul closes, as he ever does, with grace: "the grace (free favour, the whole sum of gospel blessing) of our Lord Jesus Christ" — the benediction that seals every letter; he had begun with grace (1:2) and ends with it.

On "be with you all," the witnesses note the breadth. Barnes and Ellicott note the comprehensive "with you all" — the parting blessing embraces the whole church, every member without exception, including even the disorderly he had just disciplined; grace for all.

On "Amen," the eastern Cyril notes the seal: "Amen" — the heart's confirmation, committing the church to the grace of Christ; so let it be. The verse displays the closing benediction of grace upon them all.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul closes with grace — "the grace (free favour) of our Lord Jesus Christ," the benediction sealing the letter, as he began (1:2, Barnes, Robertson); "be with you all," embracing the whole church, every member even the disciplined, grace for all (Barnes, Ellicott); "Amen," the heart's confirmation (Cyril). The verse displays the benediction. The verse marks the blessing: the grace of Christ. The verse displays the breadth: with you all. The verse displays the seal: Amen. The verse displays the close: of the epistle. The benediction displays grace invoked on the whole church. The verse displays the grace: of Christ. The verse displays the all: embraced. The verse displays the close: sealed. The closing benediction of grace upon them all displays the seal of the epistle: Paul, as he began, invokes the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ upon the whole church — every member, even the disorderly — sealing the letter, and his teaching on the day of the Lord and the man of sin, with Amen. The verse displays the grace of Christ that closes the second epistle to the Thessalonians.
